lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Aristotle, we have spoken of what **is**. But what of what **must be**? What of what **could be**?

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: Ah — you speak of the modes of truth. Some things are necessarily true: a triangle must have three sides. Others are merely contingently true: I happen to be sitting, but I could stand.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Exactly. We introduce two new operators: $\Box A$ — '$A$ is **necessary**' — and $\Diamond A$ — '$A$ is **possible**'. This is **modal logic**.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: And what are the rules governing these? Necessity seems stronger than mere truth.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: The system we shall use is called **System K** — after Saul Kripke. Its cornerstone is the **K axiom**: $\Box(A \to B) \to (\Box A \to \Box B)$. Necessity distributes over implication.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: So if it's necessary that $A$ implies $B$, and it's necessary that $A$, then it's necessary that $B$. That seems reasonable.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: The K axiom is our elimination rule for $\Box$ — the command `(box-elim N M)`. And we have a rule of **[necessitation|necessitation]** — `(box-intro N)` — which says: if you can prove $A$ with **no assumptions** (empty context), then $\Box A$ holds.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: Because if something follows from logic alone — from no contingent assumptions — then it must be necessarily true. That is elegant.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: And what of possibility $\Diamond$? It is defined in terms of necessity: $\Diamond A$ means $\lnot \Box \lnot A$ — '$A$ is possible' means 'it is not necessary that $A$ is false'.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: So we can unfold $\Diamond A$ to $\lnot \Box \lnot A$ with `(diamond-def N)`, and fold it back with `(diamond-def-rev N)`. The diamond is a derived notion.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Note an important restriction: **modal logic here is propositional**. You cannot quantify into modal contexts — no $\forall x.\; \Box P(x)$. The logic mode will be set to PL, gating the quantifier rules.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: Understood. The modal operators $\Box$ and $\Diamond$ are available, but $\forall$ and $\exists$ are not. This is System K in its pure form.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Now — what must be, must be proved. Let us explore necessity.
